flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of inserting a node at the beginning of a singly linked list?
flashcards.back: O(1) - Constant time, as we only need to update the head pointer and the new node's next pointer.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of inserting a node at the end of a singly linked list (with tail pointer)?
flashcards.back: O(1) - With a tail pointer, we directly access the last node and update it.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of inserting a node at the end WITHOUT a tail pointer?
flashcards.back: O(n) - We must traverse the entire list to reach the last node.

flashcards.front: In a singly linked list, what is the minimum number of pointers that must be tracked?
flashcards.back: Only the head pointer is mandatory. The entire list can be accessed starting from the head.

flashcards.front: What condition must be checked before deleting the last node in a singly linked list?
flashcards.back: If the list has only one node, after deletion the head pointer must be set to NULL to avoid a dangling pointer.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of searching for an element in a singly linked list?
flashcards.back: O(n) - Worst case requires traversing all n nodes. This is a linear search.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between malloc() and sizeof() when creating a new node?
flashcards.back: sizeof() returns the size of the data type (e.g., struct Node), while malloc() allocates that many bytes from heap memory.

flashcards.front: Why is it important to free memory when deleting a node in a linked list?
flashcards.back: To prevent memory leaks - the allocated heap memory must be explicitly released using free() otherwise it remains allocated until program terminates.

flashcards.front: What is a dummy head node in a singly linked list?
flashcards.back: A dummy head is a placeholder node at the beginning that simplifies insertion/deletion at position 0 by eliminating special cases for the head pointer.

flashcards.front: What is the space complexity of a singly linked list with n nodes?
flashcards.back: O(n) - Each node stores data and a pointer, so space grows linearly with the number of nodes.

flashcards.front: How do you detect a loop in a singly linked list?
flashcards.back: Use Floyd's Cycle Detection Algorithm (tortoise and hare) - two pointers moving at different speeds will meet if a cycle exists.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of deleting the first node in a singly linked list?
flashcards.back: O(1) - We simply move the head pointer to the next node and free the old head.
